source: Gaze Glass — A Family of Mortals (2).docx
heading: 7 Sonam
paragraphs: Marok descended from the clouds and glided like a flying squirrel. He yelped when he crashed into the jagged branches of a petrified  apple tree. Sonam turned her head in every direction to see where the sound came from. Sweat rolled down her chest and the back of her neck. Tears had formed under her eyes and her jaw was clenched. When her exhale finally came, she blinked and let tears of frustration roll down her cheeks. He’s such a wimp and all she does is baby him, she thought.
“Always fighting his battles” Sonam spat. “Like I’m some kind of wicked witch!”

Seconds later, a lanky mosquito hovered close to her left ear; the buzz grew louder by the second. “Get lost!” Sonam shouted and flicked her face. The mosquito avoided her fingers and floated onto one of her bushy black eyebrows. After my blood? she thought. The pest crawled onto her forehead as the wind softened Sonam’s mind to stillness. She quickly snatched one of the mosquito’s thin, long legs with her fingers and lifted the blood sucker away from her face. She twisted her face into a scowl. “Does it look like I’m in the mood for a life threatening disease?” Sonam studied the bug as it flailed and buzzed.

After a few seconds it wiggled itself free and fled. Sonam shifted her eyes quickly and smiled when the mosquito flew into a silver web nested between two dead mango trees. “Blood for blood,” Sonam chimed before wandering along the mountain path.

Marok watched Sonam kick rocks along the dirt path. She plucked flowers and gave them a single sniff before releasing them to the ground. “She’s wicked. Yes. Master will be pleased.” Marok cackled and used his divine powers to shift the terrain.

Along the way, Sonam heard water gushing. She followed the sound until she reached an unfamiliar narrow fork in the road—three paths to who knows where? I don’t remember this, she thought. Sonam folded her arms on top of each other and squeezed her elbows. Then turned her head in every direction with panic in her eyes. Calm down. You’re smart enough to figure this out. Yes, you are! she thought.

Her courage sputtered at the sight of bright, turmeric-colored eyes. High up in an oak tree, a Leopard watched without blinking. Sonam’s heart thrummed in her chest. The large cat studied her, crouched on a thick branch—and the jungle became as quiet as a cemetery. Sonam backed away, never letting her eyes drift away from the predator’s round head and hungry smile.

The wind lifted damp leaves from the ground and the clouds parted to make way for the dull, peach sunset. A loud boom of thunder sent Sonam into a frenzy. She bolted between two tall oak trees and raised her hands over her face to shield her eyes from sharp branches. The Leopard lunged into the air; its short and thick mustard-spotted fur glistened. The cat propelled forward, growling with rage, and landed with a thud.

Sonam threw her oily chin over the slope where her neck and back met. Fallen branches snapped under the Leopard's paws and leaves and flowers brushed the black spots arranged in rosettes on its back. Sonam pushed forward with ragged breaths. Wood cut into her palms and forearms like a chef’s knife moving through soft tofu.
“Help!” she screamed.

Sonam charged forward, tears forming under her eyes. She ran as fast as she could, further away from the path, and deeper into the jungle. After five minutes of speedily crawling over logs covered in mushrooms and pulling away thin vines hanging from trees, the Leopard's growls faded. All around her were giant trees, their wrinkly trunks covered in flowering vines.
“Oh my god!” she gasped. Through the canopy, she saw a bird circling the sun.

She looked down at rows of purple vines and could not find any sign of soil. Flowering plants and saplings grew everywhere. Sonam panted, held the left side of her abdomen, wincing. A gray spider—the size of a green pea—crawled across the back of her hand. Before she had a chance to scream, wood cracked and sharp growls launched her into a frenzy. Sonam jolted forward, and gasped for air. As she ran between trees, globs of mud lifted from the earth. The earth was getting softer. Anxiously, quickly, Sonam turned a corner. The Leopard bared its fangs and kicked off the ground, claws ready to tear through her.

When Sonam opened her eyes, she saw the same logs she had crawled over earlier. She studied her surroundings and smiled. Then, closed her eyes and thanked the gods for her gift—the power of foresight.
The Leopard lunged from tree to tree until it spotted Sonam.

The girl took a few steps back until her bare ankle touched mud. The feline lunged from the tree with its claws pointed at Sonam's face. She dropped to the earth and the predator landed in quicksand. It growled and hissed, pulling in every direction. Inch by inch, the earth began to swallow the feline.

The cries of desperation drew birds, rabbits, and all sorts of animals near the watery grave. A sharp, piercing pain tore through her chest, and the air began to thicken. Really? Morality? Right now? she thought. Sonam grabbed a large petrified branch off the ground and extended it over the mud.
“Take it!” Sonam shouted. “Freaking take it!”

The Leopard flailed its arms in every direction, and mud splashed across its face and into its mouth. “Come on! Take it!” Sonam screamed, tears rolling down her cheeks. The scared cat let out one last whimper before its head went below the mud. Mud and moss covered Sonam’s hands. She dragged her palms across the wrinkly bark of a barren tree and let out a sob. Tears rolled down her cheeks. You’re not the villain, she thought. It was going to eat you! Sonam stepped between fruitless trees for an hour. Her stomach growled and her tongue began to stick to the roof of her mouth. Jagged stones poked her wet black socks. The sickly stench of mold and mildew made her gag. She thought about screaming for help, but remembered the Leopard; it was unwise to draw attention in the middle of the jungle. So, instead, she hobbled along until she heard the sound of water crashing on itself, and followed the melody until she reached a large, stone wall covered in long dead vines that varied from dark red to black; they resembled streaks of lightning. Marok landed on a thick vine and cackled before diving between vines. Sonam gasped and ran to the wall. “Where did—” she started to say, inserting her index finger between crispy leaves. She wiggled her fingers before pushing her hand through. She tore down the faux wall until she stood at the entrance of a tunnel; wide enough for a car to drive through. Sunlight illuminated cracked, white limestone walls.

“Wait a minute. Was that fox—green?” Sonam asked aloud before proceeding forward. Her feet sploshed on wet black stone. With each step, the light began to fade and the scent of sandalwood became more intense. How long does this go? she thought. After all sunlight vanished, a whisper echoed through the tunnel. Sonam gasped and ran back toward the entrance. But all she found was endless black. Despite running in every direction, she couldn’t feel the walls. It was as if she had entered a void. After the whisper faded away, an orange light appeared ahead. Sonam followed it and discovered the aroma of sweet frankincense; it mixed with the scent of earthy sandalwood, resembling the incense her family burned at home. A choir of whispers swirled past as she neared the base of the wall. She studied the flickering flame and squinted. A golden wick shimmered inside of waves of blue and oranges. The flame grew three times bigger until Sonam could see a silver cylinder candle, adorned with gold flecks and pieces of sapphire sitting on a large plate made of copper. She grabbed the plate and strolled forward until she faced a tall concrete door with intricate engravings.

Sonam placed her fingers on the cold stone and traced the lines. This is Ancient Sanskrit but the artwork is Egyptian, she thought. Curiously, slowly, she recited the words on the door. The doors parted with a hiss. Cool fragrant smoke smothered her eyes. “Ugh!” she complained, squinting, until the incense thinned out, snaking around her face. The dark cave had opened into a wide corridor, lit by torches on walls. Sonam stepped inside and coughed. The white limestone walls glistened and silver light poured from the roof through intricate rows of diamond and square holes. “All you had to do was bring the boys back to the cottage,” Sonam complained and scratched an itch at the back of her neck before tiptoeing forward. After a few minutes, she reached the end of the corridor. Seven copper locks ran down the left side of a bolted stone door with hieroglyphics. No Sanskrit? she thought.
“That language came far later, mortal,”  a shrill voice said.

Sonam looked up and saw a green fox floating in the air. A green fox? This is a trauma response. Yes. Psychosis, she thought. “A jewel to ensure any mind to your will,” the fox said and cackled. Priya must have knocked me out cold, she thought. “Your sister has quite the temper,” Marok said. “Oh, I almost forgot!” Suddenly, the seven copper bolts fell to the floor. The doors parted and opened inwards. Sonam peered into the dark room and cleared her throat. “Don’t be afraid. Take a look!” the fox said, his green eyes shifted to magenta. Sonam squeezed her eyes shut. She opened them and squinted. Weird. I always wake up when I do that, she thought.
“You can talk?” Sonam asked.
“Yes. It’s just a dream after all,” Marok said. “Isn’t it?”
“It has to be! Foxes cannot talk!” Sonam confessed.
“You’re right. This must be a dream. So, go ahead! Get your prize!”
Sonam squinted. “You want me to go in there?” she asked, pointing to the abyss.

Marok’s magenta pupils melted and dripped onto the floor. Sonam backed away and watched in horror as The God of Beauty rose from the iridescent pool. The goddess glanced at the mud caked across Sonam’s hands, body, and green dress, then curled one crystal finger. Rose-gold light swept over the mortal from head to toe. The mud lifted from her skin and clothes in silken ribbons, dissolved into a cloud of glittering petals, and vanished before touching the floor. Even the dampness disappeared, leaving Sonam perfectly clean.

“Oh, Sonam,” The God of Beauty said. “Can you imagine a life where everyone listened to you?” Before Sonam could respond the goddess’ rose quartz eyes glowed. Sonam tripped over herself, the sound of her heart loud in her ears.  “Who—are—you?” the mortal girl struggled to say.  “I have many names, but do not fear. I am here to grant what your heart truly desires.”
“And what is that?” Sonam cleared her throat. “What does my heart want?”
“Control!” the God of Beauty exclaimed, laughing. “To be beautifully magnificent!”
“You’ve got it all wrong lady. I don’t want control—” Sonam started to say.
“Yes, you do. You have yet to realize it, child!” The God of Beauty snapped.

Sonam shuddered. The deity leaned forward and pressed a crystal finger against Sonam’s chest. The mortal’s exhale was as hot as steam rising from boiling water. Then, she felt as light as a feather. “Can you imagine if everyone adored your beautiful mind?” The God of Beauty asked. Sonam’s black pupils brightened into a beautiful shade of pink and the corners of her lips rose. She saw herself sitting at the dinner table with her family. As she spoke, everyone smiled and clapped. “More than … anything,” Sonam said slowly, her teary eyes bulged in awe. The God of Beauty lifted her finger and touched the space between Sonam’s brows and the mortal saw a life that could be: she stood in front of a boardroom in a black suit and received a standing ovation, accepted pink roses from a handsome man with an eyepatch, and clapped as Rashid married Elijah. “It’s so beautiful—” Sonam began, tears forming under her eyes.

“A beautiful future is lovely, but to achieve it! You need power! It is in that room!” At the God of Beauty’s words, Sonam’s eyes shifted back to brown. She entered the darkness. Ancient candles hanging on limestone walls ignited as the girl stepped into the room. A round table made of purple Amethyst drew the candlelight and Sonam’s attention. “What is this place?” Sonam asked as wind lifted her curls. When she focused on the shimmer, all light surrounding her faded. She wandered to the table and tilted her chin down and peered into the sparkling crystal. Like a well with water, Sonam eyed a blue stone at the base of the table; it looked nearly fifteen feet away from the surface.

Suddenly, a black cobra slithered across Sonam’s foot and hissed, causing her to shriek and fall on top of the Amethyst. The purple surface turned into snow and spilled in every direction. Sonam flailed in the ice and shivered. Then she saw the serpent slither up the wall. Suddenly, ancient whispers crawled over each other and from corners.

“Embrace a voice that gives no choice, but I warn you—there are rules—and a price to pay,” the God of Beauty warned with a sickly sweet voice. “But you’ll gain such delicious boons and blessings.” Sonam did not heed her warning. She shoved her hand into the snow and felt soft ice fill the space between her fingers. When the tip of her thumb grazed something solid, she tapped it. Icy air rushed out from her lungs.
The God of Beauty floated and let the black cobra slither onto her shoulder.

“They say any soul will do anything for the one they love the most!” the God of Beauty exclaimed as a bright blue downward pointing triangle with a circle inside formed on Sonam’s throat. Sonam blinked. A second later, she was surrounded by wilting trees and colorful saplings at her feet. She blinked again. Then, she shook her head and wiggled her fingers.
“This heat is making me crazy.”
